scenarioDescription: A small business wants to host a simple static website with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files. The site needs to be globally accessible with low latency and must be cost-effective since they expect moderate traffic.
toolboxServices: Amazon S3|Amazon CloudFront|Amazon EC2|Amazon RDS|AWS Lambda|Amazon Route 53|Amazon EBS
hint: Static files like HTML and CSS can be stored as objects in a highly durable storage service without needing a server.
hint2: A content delivery network caches your static content at edge locations worldwide for low-latency access.
explanation: Amazon S3 hosts the static website files (HTML, CSS, JS) as objects with static website hosting enabled — no server management required. Amazon CloudFront distributes the content globally through edge locations, reducing latency for users worldwide. Amazon Route 53 provides DNS resolution, mapping the custom domain name to the CloudFront distribution for a professional web address.

scenarioDescription: A startup needs to run a web application with unpredictable traffic spikes. They want servers that automatically scale up during peak hours and scale down at night to minimize costs, with traffic distributed evenly across all running servers.
toolboxServices: Amazon EC2|Auto Scaling Group|Elastic Load Balancing|Amazon S3|AWS Lambda|Amazon RDS|Amazon Lightsail
correctPipelineOrder: Elastic Load Balancing|Auto Scaling Group|Amazon EC2
hint: Incoming user requests first hit a service that distributes traffic across multiple targets.
hint2: A scaling service automatically adjusts the number of compute instances based on demand metrics.
explanation: Elastic Load Balancing receives incoming traffic and distributes it evenly across healthy EC2 instances. The Auto Scaling Group automatically launches or terminates EC2 instances based on demand, scaling out during traffic spikes and scaling in during quiet periods. Amazon EC2 provides the virtual servers that actually run the web application code.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to store customer profile data that is frequently read and written. The data is structured with relationships between customers, orders, and products. They need ACID-compliant transactions and the ability to run complex SQL queries for reporting.
toolboxServices: Amazon RDS|Amazon DynamoDB|Amazon S3|Amazon Redshift|Amazon ElastiCache|Amazon Aurora|Amazon Neptune
hint: Structured data with relationships and SQL queries points to a relational database, not a NoSQL or object store.
hint2: AWS offers a managed relational database service that handles patching, backups, and maintenance automatically.
explanation: Amazon RDS is the correct choice because the scenario requires a relational database with ACID transactions, structured data with relationships, and complex SQL queries. RDS is a fully managed service that supports engines like MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server, handling backups, patching, and scaling. DynamoDB would be wrong because it is NoSQL and does not natively support complex SQL joins, while Redshift is optimized for analytics warehousing, not transactional workloads.

scenarioDescription: An e-commerce company wants to send order confirmation emails to customers immediately after purchase. The email sending function should only run when triggered and the company should not pay for idle compute time. Order details are stored in a database.
toolboxServices: AWS Lambda|Amazon SNS|Amazon SQS|Amazon EC2|Amazon DynamoDB|Amazon SES|AWS Step Functions
hint: Order details need to be read from a fast, scalable NoSQL database before the email can be composed.
hint2: The compute service that runs code without provisioning servers and charges only for execution time is serverless.
explanation: Amazon DynamoDB stores the order details as the source of truth, providing fast reads at any scale. AWS Lambda is triggered by the order event, retrieves the order details from DynamoDB, and composes the email — it runs only when invoked and charges per execution, eliminating idle costs. Amazon SES (Simple Email Service) sends the actual confirmation email to the customer reliably and cost-effectively.

scenarioDescription: A hospital needs to store patient medical records that must be retained for 7 years by regulation. The records are rarely accessed after the first 90 days but must remain retrievable within 12 hours when needed. Cost optimization is the top priority.
toolboxServices: Amazon S3 Standard|Amazon S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval|Amazon S3 Glacier Deep Archive|Amazon EBS|Amazon EFS|S3 Lifecycle Policy|Amazon S3 Intelligent-Tiering
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3 Standard|S3 Lifecycle Policy|Amazon S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval
hint: New records need immediate access, so they start in a storage class designed for frequently accessed data.
hint2: An automated policy can transition objects between storage classes based on age without manual intervention.
explanation: Patient records are initially stored in Amazon S3 Standard for immediate access during the first 90 days when they are frequently referenced. An S3 Lifecycle Policy automatically transitions records to a cheaper storage class after 90 days without manual intervention. Amazon S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval provides the lowest-cost archival storage with retrieval times of 1-12 hours, meeting both the 7-year retention requirement and the 12-hour retrieval SLA at minimal cost.

scenarioDescription: A mobile gaming company needs a database that can handle millions of read/write requests per second with single-digit millisecond latency. The data model is simple key-value pairs storing player scores and game state, and the schema may change frequently.
toolboxServices: Amazon DynamoDB|Amazon RDS|Amazon Aurora|Amazon ElastiCache|Amazon Redshift|Amazon Neptune|Amazon S3
hint: Key-value data with flexible schemas and extreme scale points to a specific type of NoSQL database.
hint2: This fully managed database automatically scales throughput and provides consistent single-digit millisecond performance.
explanation: Amazon DynamoDB is the correct choice because it is a fully managed NoSQL key-value database designed for exactly this use case — millions of requests per second with single-digit millisecond latency and flexible schemas. RDS and Aurora are relational databases that would struggle with this scale of simple key-value operations and require fixed schemas. ElastiCache is an in-memory cache, not a durable primary database for persistent game state.

scenarioDescription: A company wants to migrate their on-premises VMware virtual machines to AWS. They need to discover their existing server inventory, plan the migration, and then perform the actual server migration with minimal downtime to their production environment.
toolboxServices: AWS Application Discovery Service|AWS Migration Hub|AWS Application Migration Service|AWS Database Migration Service|AWS Snow Family|Amazon EC2|AWS Transfer Family
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Application Discovery Service|AWS Migration Hub|AWS Application Migration Service
hint: Before migrating anything, you must first discover and inventory what servers and dependencies exist on-premises.
hint2: A central dashboard tracks the progress of all migration activities across multiple AWS migration tools.
explanation: AWS Application Discovery Service scans the on-premises environment to inventory servers, collect performance data, and map dependencies. AWS Migration Hub provides a centralized dashboard to plan and track the migration progress across all discovered servers. AWS Application Migration Service (MGN) performs the actual lift-and-shift migration of the VMware VMs to EC2 instances with minimal downtime using continuous block-level replication.

scenarioDescription: A financial company needs to ensure that all their AWS resources comply with internal security policies. They want to automatically detect when an S3 bucket is made public, when security groups allow unrestricted SSH access, and receive alerts when resources drift from approved configurations.
toolboxServices: AWS Config|AWS CloudTrail|Amazon GuardDuty|AWS Security Hub|Amazon Inspector|AWS Trusted Advisor|Amazon Macie
hint: One service continuously monitors and records the configuration state of your AWS resources against rules.
hint2: A centralized security service aggregates findings from multiple AWS security tools into a single dashboard.
explanation: AWS Config continuously monitors and records the configuration of AWS resources, evaluating them against predefined rules (like detecting public S3 buckets or open SSH security groups) and flagging non-compliant resources automatically. AWS Security Hub aggregates the Config findings along with alerts from other security services into a centralized dashboard with compliance scores. CloudTrail logs API calls but does not evaluate compliance, and GuardDuty detects threats rather than configuration drift.

scenarioDescription: A media company needs to process uploaded video files by extracting thumbnails, transcoding to multiple formats, and generating subtitles. Each step depends on the previous one completing successfully, and failed steps should retry automatically before alerting the operations team.
toolboxServices: AWS Step Functions|AWS Lambda|Amazon S3|Amazon SQS|Amazon SNS|AWS Batch|Amazon EventBridge
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|AWS Step Functions|AWS Lambda|Amazon SNS
hint: Uploaded video files land in object storage, which triggers the processing workflow.
hint2: A workflow orchestration service coordinates multiple steps with built-in error handling and retry logic.
explanation: Videos are uploaded to Amazon S3, which triggers the processing pipeline. AWS Step Functions orchestrates the multi-step workflow (thumbnail extraction → transcoding → subtitle generation) with built-in retry logic, error handling, and conditional branching for each dependent step. AWS Lambda executes the actual processing code for each step as serverless functions. Amazon SNS sends alerts to the operations team when retries are exhausted and a step permanently fails.

scenarioDescription: A company wants to give their mobile app users the ability to sign up, sign in, and access AWS resources securely. They need social login support (Google, Facebook), multi-factor authentication, and temporary AWS credentials for authenticated users to access S3 buckets.
toolboxServices: Amazon Cognito|AWS IAM|AWS IAM Identity Center|AWS Directory Service|Amazon GuardDuty|AWS Secrets Manager|AWS STS
hint: AWS offers a managed service specifically designed for adding user sign-up and sign-in to mobile and web applications.
hint2: After authentication, temporary security credentials are needed so mobile users can access AWS resources directly.
explanation: Amazon Cognito handles user sign-up, sign-in, social identity federation (Google, Facebook), and MFA for the mobile app — it is purpose-built for this use case. AWS STS (Security Token Service) provides temporary, limited-privilege AWS credentials to authenticated Cognito users, following the principle of least privilege. These temporary credentials allow the mobile app to access Amazon S3 buckets directly without embedding long-term access keys in the application.

scenarioDescription: A retail company wants to analyze 5 years of historical sales data to identify trends, seasonal patterns, and generate executive dashboards. The data is currently in CSV files totaling 10 TB and they need to run complex analytical queries across the entire dataset.
toolboxServices: Amazon Redshift|Amazon RDS|Amazon DynamoDB|Amazon Athena|Amazon S3|Amazon QuickSight|AWS Glue
hint: The raw CSV files need to be stored in a scalable, cost-effective data lake before any processing begins.
hint2: An ETL service transforms and loads the raw data into a data warehouse optimized for analytical queries.
explanation: The 10 TB of CSV files are stored in Amazon S3 as the data lake foundation. AWS Glue performs ETL (extract, transform, load) to clean, transform, and catalog the data before loading it into the warehouse. Amazon Redshift is the purpose-built data warehouse designed for complex analytical queries across petabytes of structured data. Amazon QuickSight creates the interactive executive dashboards and visualizations from the Redshift query results.

scenarioDescription: A development team needs to deploy a web application quickly without managing servers, load balancers, or scaling configurations. They just want to upload their code and have AWS handle all the infrastructure provisioning, capacity management, and health monitoring automatically.
toolboxServices: AWS Elastic Beanstalk|Amazon EC2|Amazon ECS|AWS Lambda|AWS CloudFormation|Amazon Lightsail|AWS App Runner
hint: This service is a PaaS (Platform as a Service) that automatically provisions EC2 instances, load balancers, and auto-scaling behind the scenes.
hint2: Developers upload their application code, and this service handles all infrastructure decisions automatically.
explanation: AWS Elastic Beanstalk is the correct answer because it is a fully managed PaaS that automatically handles infrastructure provisioning, load balancing, auto-scaling, and health monitoring — developers simply upload their code. Unlike EC2 (which requires manual server management) or Lambda (which is for event-driven functions, not traditional web apps), Elastic Beanstalk provides the easiest path to deploy a full web application while still allowing infrastructure customization if needed later.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to securely store database passwords, API keys, and third-party service credentials used by their applications. The credentials must be automatically rotated on a schedule, encrypted at rest, and accessible only to authorized applications through fine-grained IAM policies.
toolboxServices: AWS Secrets Manager|AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store|AWS KMS|AWS IAM|Amazon S3|AWS Certificate Manager|AWS CloudHSM
hint: AWS offers a dedicated service for managing secrets like database credentials with built-in automatic rotation.
hint2: The secrets are encrypted at rest using a key management service that controls the encryption keys.
explanation: AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built for storing, managing, and automatically rotating secrets like database passwords, API keys, and credentials with fine-grained IAM access control. AWS KMS (Key Management Service) provides the encryption keys that Secrets Manager uses to encrypt the secrets at rest, ensuring they are protected with AWS-managed or customer-managed encryption keys. Parameter Store is a lighter alternative but lacks native automatic rotation capabilities for database credentials.

scenarioDescription: A company wants to run Docker containers in production without managing the underlying EC2 instances, operating systems, or cluster infrastructure. They want to define their containers and let AWS handle all server provisioning, patching, and scaling of the compute layer.
toolboxServices: AWS Fargate|Amazon ECS|Amazon EKS|Amazon EC2|AWS Lambda|Amazon ECR|AWS App Runner
hint: Container images must first be stored in a registry before they can be deployed.
hint2: A serverless compute engine for containers eliminates the need to manage EC2 instances underneath your container orchestrator.
explanation: Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry) stores the Docker container images securely. Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service) provides the container orchestration layer that defines tasks, services, and how containers should run. AWS Fargate is the serverless compute engine that runs the ECS containers without requiring you to provision, manage, or patch any EC2 instances — AWS handles all the underlying infrastructure automatically.

scenarioDescription: A company wants to track all API calls made across their AWS account for security auditing and compliance. They need to know who made each API call, when it was made, from which IP address, and what resources were affected. The logs must be stored immutably for 1 year.
toolboxServices: AWS CloudTrail|Amazon CloudWatch|AWS Config|VPC Flow Logs|Amazon S3|AWS GuardDuty|AWS Audit Manager
hint: One service records every API call made in your AWS account as an event log — think of it as the security camera for your AWS account.
hint2: The audit logs need to be stored in durable, immutable storage for the 1-year retention requirement.
explanation: AWS CloudTrail records every API call made in the AWS account, capturing who made the call, when, from which IP address, and what resources were affected — it is the definitive audit trail for AWS activity. The CloudTrail logs are delivered to Amazon S3 for durable, long-term storage with S3 Object Lock enabling immutable retention for the 1-year compliance requirement. CloudWatch monitors metrics and alarms but does not record API call details, and VPC Flow Logs capture network traffic, not API calls.

scenarioDescription: A company wants to define their entire AWS infrastructure — VPCs, EC2 instances, RDS databases, and IAM roles — as code in template files. They need to deploy identical environments for development, staging, and production with a single command and roll back changes if deployments fail.
toolboxServices: AWS CloudFormation|AWS CDK|Terraform|AWS Elastic Beanstalk|AWS OpsWorks|AWS Systems Manager|AWS CodeDeploy
hint: This AWS-native service uses JSON or YAML template files to define and provision infrastructure as code.
hint2: It creates 'stacks' of resources that can be replicated across environments and automatically rolled back on failure.
explanation: AWS CloudFormation is the correct answer because it is the AWS-native Infrastructure as Code (IaC) service that uses JSON or YAML templates to define all AWS resources declaratively. It creates stacks that can be deployed identically across dev, staging, and production environments with a single command, and it automatically rolls back all changes if any resource in the stack fails to create. Elastic Beanstalk deploys applications (not arbitrary infrastructure), and CodeDeploy handles application deployments to existing servers, not infrastructure provisioning.

scenarioDescription: A news website experiences a massive traffic spike whenever breaking news occurs. Their database becomes the bottleneck because the same popular articles are read millions of times. They need to reduce database load by caching frequently accessed data in memory with microsecond response times.
toolboxServices: Amazon ElastiCache|Amazon DynamoDB|Amazon RDS|Amazon CloudFront|Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX)|Amazon MemoryDB|Amazon S3
hint: An in-memory caching layer sits in front of the database to serve repeated reads without hitting the database.
hint2: This managed service supports Redis and Memcached engines for microsecond-latency data retrieval.
explanation: Amazon ElastiCache is placed in front of the database as an in-memory caching layer that stores frequently accessed article data, serving millions of repeated reads with microsecond latency without touching the database. Amazon RDS remains the primary database storing the authoritative article data, but now only handles cache misses instead of every single read request. CloudFront caches static content at edge locations but cannot cache dynamic database query results like ElastiCache can.

scenarioDescription: A company is evaluating the total cost of running their workloads on AWS before migrating. They need to estimate monthly costs for specific EC2 instance types, S3 storage, and RDS databases, and then set up billing alerts to avoid unexpected charges after deployment.
toolboxServices: AWS Pricing Calculator|AWS Cost Explorer|AWS Budgets|AWS Trusted Advisor|AWS Cost and Usage Report|Amazon CloudWatch|AWS Billing Dashboard
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Pricing Calculator|AWS Budgets|AWS Cost Explorer
hint: Before deploying anything, use a free tool to estimate monthly costs based on the specific services and configurations you plan to use.
hint2: After deployment, set spending thresholds that trigger email alerts when your actual costs approach your budget.
explanation: AWS Pricing Calculator is used before migration to estimate monthly costs by configuring specific EC2 instance types, S3 storage volumes, and RDS configurations to get a projected bill. AWS Budgets sets custom spending thresholds and sends email/SNS alerts when actual or forecasted costs exceed the defined budget, preventing surprise charges. AWS Cost Explorer provides detailed cost analysis after deployment, showing spending trends, breakdowns by service, and optimization recommendations over time.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to build a loosely coupled microservices architecture where services communicate asynchronously. When the order service creates a new order, the inventory service, shipping service, and notification service all need to receive and process that event independently.
toolboxServices: Amazon SNS|Amazon SQS|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Lambda|Amazon MQ|Amazon Kinesis|AWS Step Functions
hint: A pub/sub messaging service can broadcast a single message to multiple subscribers simultaneously.
hint2: Each subscribing microservice needs its own message queue to process events at its own pace without losing messages.
explanation: Amazon SNS (Simple Notification Service) acts as the pub/sub topic where the order service publishes new order events — it fans out each message to all subscribers simultaneously. Amazon SQS (Simple Queue Service) queues are subscribed to the SNS topic, giving each microservice (inventory, shipping, notification) its own independent queue to process messages at its own pace. This SNS-to-SQS fanout pattern is the standard AWS approach for loosely coupled, asynchronous microservice communication.

scenarioDescription: A company wants to protect their web application from common attacks like SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), and DDoS attacks. They also need to block requests from specific countries and rate-limit API calls from individual IP addresses that exceed 1000 requests per minute.
toolboxServices: AWS WAF|AWS Shield|Amazon GuardDuty|AWS Firewall Manager|Security Groups|Network ACLs|Amazon CloudFront
hint: A CDN edge layer is the first line of defense, absorbing traffic before it reaches your origin servers.
hint2: A web application firewall inspects HTTP requests and blocks those matching SQL injection and XSS patterns.
explanation: Amazon CloudFront serves as the first layer of defense at the edge, absorbing and distributing traffic globally before it reaches origin servers. AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall) is attached to CloudFront and inspects every HTTP request, blocking SQL injection, XSS attacks, requests from restricted countries using geo-match rules, and rate-limiting IPs exceeding 1000 requests per minute. AWS Shield provides DDoS protection — the Standard tier is automatic and free with CloudFront, while the Advanced tier adds enhanced detection and 24/7 DDoS response team support.
